docs.title: PRISM Candidate Scoring System
docs.text:
10 min read · Last updated April 04, 2026 · 2,062 words

PRISM is FORGE's technical candidate evaluator, a port of the glassattempt Chrome extension logic into the Discord bot. It scores candidates against a job description (JD) on a 1-5 scale (0.5 increments) using a three-part framework: technical fit (60%), environment fit (25%), and role-specific signals (15%).

docs.title: Contents
docs.text:
1. Overview
  2. Scoring Framework
  3. PRISM V2 System Prompt
  4. Leadership Detection
  5. Tech Stack Matching
  6. FastEval vs V2 Batch
  7. Outreach Integration
  8. Key Files

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
PRISM evaluates candidates by answering three core questions: 1. Can they do the job on day 1-30? (Technical Fit — 60%) 2. Will they thrive here? (Environment Fit — 25%) 3. Do they meet JD-specific requirements? (Role Signals — 15%)

The scoring is depth-driven, not keyword-driven. A candidate who lists "AI Agents" because they built one RAG chatbot is NOT an agent engineer. Real experience is measured by: - What they actually built (not what they list) - At what companies (tech company vs consulting vs academic) - At what scale (production shipping vs course project)

The system enforces a critical distinction between hard-to-learn skills (domain expertise, system design patterns) and easy-to-learn skills (specific languages, frameworks). A strong backend engineer missing a specific language is far more valuable than someone who knows the language but has never built the type of system the JD describes.

docs.title: 1-5 Scale with 0.5 Increments
docs.text: Score Definition 5 Technical bullseye + environment fit — top candidate in any pipeline 4.5 Strong technical match + clear environment fit — worth fast-tracking 4 Good technical match, may need ramp on 1-2 easy-to-learn areas — solid interview candidate 3.5 Partial technical match OR strong adjacent experience that transfers — worth a screen 3 Relevant background but missing key requirements from the JD 2.5 Tangentially related — would need significant ramp 2 General engineer, minimal overlap with what the JD asks for 1.5 Wrong domain entirely or too junior to be useful 1 No relevance to the role

docs.title: Weighting Breakdown
docs.text:
Technical Fit (60%) — hands-on experience with JD technologies, production shipping history, real depth vs surface-level keywords

Environment Fit (25%) — startup/early-stage experience, pace signals (shipped fast, wore many hats, 0-to-1 building), trajectory indicators

Role Signals (15%) — location, seniority, domain expertise, any explicit JD requirements

docs.title: Key Scoring Rules
docs.text:
1. Score based on what the JD asks for, not what seems impressive
  2. Degree field does not matter — judge by what they have BUILT, not what they studied
  3. Startup experience is a tiebreaker, not a score booster. Two equal candidates? The startup person wins. But startup DNA alone doesn't compensate for missing core tech
  4. Big-company-only experience is a soft negative for early-stage roles, not a hard reject
  5. Past founder/CTO/CEO at small startups are POSITIVE signals if they returned to IC (Individual Contributor) work. Evaluate what they did and what they're doing NOW
  6. Internships don't count as real experience — only full-time roles. Interns-only profiles cap at 2, max 2.5 if exceptional
  7. Degree field irrelevant. Math, physics, self-taught — judge by what they built
  8. Years of experience: use the JD's seniority signal. Don't penalize senior people unless the JD explicitly targets junior candidates

docs.title: PRISM V2 System Prompt
docs.text: The V2 prompt is a unified instruction set injected into Claude's system context when evaluating candidates. It defines the scoring framework, rules, and critical depth-vs-keywords distinction.

docs.title: Key Sections
docs.text:
DEPTH vs KEYWORDS (CRITICAL)

This is the most important section. It explicitly warns against keyword-matching:

Having a keyword on your profile is NOT the same as having real experience. Someone who lists 'AI Agents' because they built one RAG chatbot is NOT an agent engineer.

Look at WHAT they actually built, at WHAT companies, at WHAT scale. A 'Solutions Engineer' or 'AI Solutions' title at a data labeling company is NOT software engineering.

5 means you would bet money this person can walk in and BUILD the systems the JD describes. Not that they have touched similar tools. BUILT, SHIPPED, IN PRODUCTION, AT SCALE.

Hard-to-Learn vs Easy-to-Learn Skills

The prompt emphasizes that specific languages and frameworks are easy to learn, but domain expertise and system design patterns are hard:

Distinguish between HARD-TO-LEARN skills (domain expertise, system design patterns) and EASY-TO-LEARN skills (specific languages, frameworks). A strong backend engineer missing a specific language is far more valuable than someone who knows the language but has never built the type of system the JD describes.

Company Context Signals

Non-tech companies and roles carry lower weight:

Non-profit, government, academic, and consulting roles are weaker signals than shipping production software at a tech company. Weight accordingly.

docs.title: Leadership Detection
docs.text: PRISM includes a local, regex-based leadership detection engine that identifies leadership signals without API calls. This runs before the main evaluation for roles marked as "leadership" in the JD.

docs.title: Detected Signals
docs.text:
The leadership engine uses pattern matching to find:

  • Team Management (led a team of X, managed X engineers)
  • Mentorship (mentored X engineers, mentored junior developers)
  • Hiring (hired, recruited, onboarded engineers)
  • Team Growth (grew team from X to Y)
  • Process/Standards (established engineering practices, defined technical direction)
  • 0→1 Building (built from scratch, greenfield)
  • Founding Engineer (founding engineer, first engineer, employee #X)
  • Early Employee (early engineer, early employee)
  • Cross-Functional Work (cross-functional collaboration, worked with product/design)
  • Architecture Ownership (designed, architected, owned system design)
  • Title Progression (Junior → Engineer → Senior → Staff → Lead/Manager)

Each signal carries a weighted point value. For example: - Founding engineer: +25 points - Managed X engineers: +15 points - Early engineer: +20 points - Title progression 3+ levels: +20 points

Maximum leadership score: 100 points.

docs.title: Leadership Report
docs.text:
For leadership roles, PRISM also generates: - Leadership Score (out of 100) - Startup Fit Score (out of 100) - Tech Stack Match (from JD technologies) - Recommendation (Strong/Moderate/Weak/Not Recommended fit)

Example recommendation thresholds: - Leadership ≥70 + Startup ≥50: Strong Fit - Leadership ≥50 + Startup ≥30: Moderate Fit (verify in interview) - Leadership ≥30: Weak Fit (better for IC role) - Leadership <30: Not Recommended for leadership role

docs.title: Tech Stack Matching
docs.text: PRISM dynamically extracts technologies from the JD and matches them against the candidate's profile. This is not hardcoded — the tech list is built from the JD content using regex patterns with word boundaries.

docs.title: Supported Technologies
docs.text:
Languages: Node.js, TypeScript, JavaScript, Python, Go, Rust, Java, C++, Ruby, Scala, Kotlin

Frontend: React, Vue.js, Angular, Next.js

Backend: FastAPI, Flask, Django, Express

Data: ClickHouse, PostgreSQL, MySQL, MongoDB, Redis, Elasticsearch, Kafka, DynamoDB

Cloud: AWS, GCP, Azure

Infrastructure: Docker, Kubernetes, Terraform

AI/ML: LangChain, LLM, OpenAI, Anthropic, Braintrust, Cerebras, PyTorch, TensorFlow, ChromaDB, Pinecone

docs.title: Matching Algorithm
docs.text:
1. Extract all tech keywords from JD using regex patterns (with word boundaries to avoid false matches)
  2. Search candidate profile for each technology
  3. Calculate match percentage: found / total
  4. Assign match level:
  5. Strong ≥75% of JD technologies found
  6. Moderate 40-74% found
  7. Weak <40% found

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
JD mentions: Node.js, TypeScript, PostgreSQL, React, Docker

Candidate has: Node.js, TypeScript, Docker

Result: Moderate Match (3/5 = 60%)

docs.title: FastEval vs V2 Batch
docs.text: PRISM supports two evaluation modes, optimized for different workflows.

docs.title: FastEval (Single Candidate)
docs.text:
Used for individual candidate evaluation. Generates a detailed report with:

Score: [1-5 with 0.5 increments] / 5

Justification:
[One short paragraph explaining overall fit]

Key Points:
- Bullet 1
- Bullet 2
- Bullet 3
- Bullet 4

Why not a 5:
[One or two sentences explaining what held them back]

Sponsorship Check:
Sponsorship Confidence Score: XX%
[Brief explanation of visa sponsorship likelihood]

Temperature: 0 (deterministic) Max tokens: 2048

docs.title: V2 Batch (Multiple Candidates)
docs.text:
Used for bulk evaluation of 10+ candidates. Outputs JSON only, one object per candidate:

[
  { "i": 0, "s": 4.5, "r": "strong technical match", "p": "personalization" },
  { "i": 1, "s": 3.0, "r": "relevant but missing key reqs" },
  { "i": 2, "s": 2.0, "r": "minimal overlap" }
]

Fields: - i — candidate index (from input order) - s — score (1-5 with 0.5 increments) - r — reason (max 15 words) - p — personalization (1-2 sentences as Arda, CTO) — only for 4+ scores

Automatic Filter: Candidates at their current company <6 months score 1.0 (not reachable).

Personalization Format (for 4+ scores only): - Direct and specific to their profile - Reference GitHub repos if available, else company/technical work - 1-2 sentences - No greetings or sign-offs - Written as if from Arda (the CTO voice)

Example: "Your ClickHouse work at Databricks + TypeScript depth is exactly what we need. That scale of data pipeline experience is rare."

docs.title: Outreach Integration
docs.text: For candidates scoring 3.5 or higher, FORGE automatically generates personalized outreach messages. The system uses a two-stage approach:

docs.title: Stage 1: Category Classification
docs.text:
The outreach_personalizer module classifies candidates into one of 8 personalization categories using rule-based decision trees:

  1. Active GitHub Builder — 10+ public repos, starred repos
  2. Open Source Contributor — forked repos, contributed
  3. Strong Company Pedigree — top-tier company (Google, Meta, Stripe, etc.)
  4. Direct Skill Match — matches JD technologies
  5. YC/Startup Network — Y Combinator or venture-backed company
  6. Elite Education — Stanford, MIT, CMU, etc.
  7. Working on Interesting Product — notable technical work at current company
  8. Recently Changed Roles — role change within 6 months
  9. Default — strong background, generic

docs.title: Stage 2: Template Filling
docs.text: Human-written templates for each category are filled with candidate-specific details using Claude Haiku. Output is a 1-2 sentence personalization injected as an Ashby sequence variable.

docs.title: Outreach Quality Gate
docs.text:
Before sending ANY outreach message, the system performs 25-rule validation:

Hard Bans (fix before sending): - NO em dashes (—) anywhere — use commas, periods, or parentheses - NO "Worth a chat/conversation/call" - NO "on your radar" - NO "I came across your profile" / "I was impressed by" / "caught my eye" - NO filler sentences ("That's a pretty unique combo", "That's exactly the kind of...") - NO time commitments ("15 min", "quick call", "30 min") - NO restating resume back at them

Format Rules: - 1-2 sentences MAX - Blurb only — no "Hi [Name]" wrapper (Ashby template handles that) - No CTA — personalization is the opener - Close with "Happy to share more if you're curious" or "Open to chatting if you are" - OR ask a genuine question instead of CTA

Example (Good)

Saw that you led the infrastructure team at Stripe building their rate-limiting system — that's the exact scale of problem we're solving at HockeyStack. If you're ever curious about shaping GTM infrastructure from scratch, happy to chat.

Example (Bad)

I came across your profile and was really impressed by your work at Stripe. We're building something similar and I think you'd be a great fit. Would love to grab a quick 15-min call to chat more about it.

docs.title: PrizePicks Sports Model
docs.text:
10 min read · Last updated April 04, 2026 · 1,997 words

The PrizePicks Sports Model is FORGE's multi-sport player prop analysis and grading system. It combines live projections from the PrizePicks partner API, real-time player statistics from official sports data sources (NBA Stats, NHL API, VLR), and a 4-agent swarm evaluation system powered by Claude Haiku. The model currently supports NBA, NHL, MLB, WNBA, NFL, and Valorant esports.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
FORGE continuously monitors player prop lines from the PrizePicks partner API and identifies potential edges by comparing: - PrizePicks line vs. player's recent performance (L10 average, median, weighted projection) - High-confidence stat categories (e.g., Points, Rebounds, Assists for NBA; Kills for Valorant) - Variance analysis (standard deviation, hit rates, trend detection)

When an edge is found, the model uses a 4-agent swarm evaluator that assesses the pick from four independent perspectives (Stats, Matchup, Trends, Contrarian), then a Judge synthesizes the opinions into a final verdict with confidence score. Picks are graded automatically as game results become available.

Key Metrics (as of Mar 2026): - Overall accuracy: 70.2% across all sports - Under picks: 85.2% accuracy (slight model advantage) - Over picks: ~60% accuracy - 57+ predictions graded in the sports_predictions table - Cost: FREE (uses only free APIs)

docs.title: Data Sources
docs.text:
• PrizePicks Partner API (https://partner-api.prizepicks.com)
  • Fetches current projections and lines for all supported sports
  • Uses league IDs: NBA=7, NHL=9, MLB=2, WNBA=3, NFL=1, Valorant=159

  • No authentication required

  • Stats APIs (sport-specific)

  • NBA: nba_api Python package (stats.nba.com)
  • NHL: api-web.nhle.com official REST API
  • Valorant: vlrdevapi package (scrapes VLR.gg)

docs.title: Line Selection Strategy
docs.text:
Standard Line Selection: PrizePicks posts the original line first, then adds variant lines (demon/goblin alternates) with slightly different odds at later timestamps.

The model uses standard_only=True to isolate the canonical line: 1. Group all projections by (player, stat_type) tuple 2. Sort each group by board_time (earliest = original) 3. Keep only the first (standard) line per player+stat

This filters ~250 raw lines down to ~100-150 actionable standard lines per sport query.

High-Confidence Stats: Only models stats with predictable patterns: - NBA: Points, Rebounds, Assists, Pts+Rebs+Asts, 3-Pt Made, Blks+Stls, Turnovers, Fantasy Score - NHL: Points, Shots On Goal, Goals, Assists, Blocked Shots, Saves, Goalies - Saves, Fantasy Score - MLB: Total Bases, Hits+Runs+RBIs, Pitcher Strikeouts, Hits Allowed, Walks Allowed, Fantasy Score

docs.title: Median vs. Mean
docs.text: The model calculates both L10 average and L10 median because: - Averages can be skewed by outlier games (player with 45 points in a blowout) - Medians better reflect consistent baseline performance - High standard deviation signals low predictability

docs.title: Weighted Projection
docs.text:
Recent games weighted heavier than older games: 

Weighted Avg = (G1×w1 + G2×w2 + ... + G10×w10) / sum(weights)
Weights decay: most recent game = 1.0, oldest = ~0.3

docs.title: Hit Rate Calculation
docs.text:
For each stat, tracks: - Hit Rate Over: % of games player exceeded the line - Hit Rate Under: % of games player fell short - Edge %: (player_avg - line) / line × 100

Example: Player averages 25.3 pts, line is 24.5, edge = +3.3% OVER

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
The swarm is a 5-model consensus system that evaluates each pick in parallel:

       PICK (player, stat, line, direction)
            |
         [PARALLEL AGENTS - 4 Haiku calls]
            |
    +-------+-------+-------+--------+
    |       |       |       |        |
   Stats  Matchup Trends Contrarian [Judge]
  Analyst Analyst Analyst  Analyst   |
    |       |       |       |        |
    +-------+-------+-------+--------+
            |
       FINAL VERDICT
    (OVER/UNDER/SKIP, confidence 0-100)

docs.title: Agent Roles
docs.text:
1. Stats Analyst — Pure quantitative analysis - Compares L10 average vs. line gap - Assesses hit rate consistency - Flags high variance as unpredictable - Weights: median, standard deviation, sample size - Verdict: OVER, UNDER, or SKIP

2. Matchup Analyst — Context from opponent + situational factors - Research opponent's defensive rank for this stat - Account for pace of play (fast teams inflate stats) - Consider position-specific matchups - Flag special circumstances (rival, playoff implications) - Verdict: OVER, UNDER, or SKIP

3. Trends Analyst — Momentum and situational patterns - Hot streak detection: recent games > L10 avg → trending up - Rest patterns: back-to-back games = ~3-5% stat decline - Home vs. away splits - Fatigue and motivation signals (time of season) - Verdict: OVER, UNDER, or SKIP

4. Contrarian Analyst — Devil's advocate role - Identifies "trap lines" (lines that look too easy) - Regression to the mean: hot/cold streaks revert - Sample size bias: 10 games is small, anything can happen - Blowout risk: player benched if team is up/down 30+ - Questions if public consensus is wrong - Verdict: AGREE (with the consensus pick), FADE (oppose it), or SKIP

docs.title: Judge
docs.text:
Synthesizes all four opinions using weighted consensus: - Stats analyst: Highest weight (40%) - Matchup analyst: Medium-high weight (30%) - Trends analyst: Medium weight (20%) - Contrarian analyst: Medium weight (20%)

If 3+ agents agree on direction → lean toward that consensus. If contrarian raises a strong concern → reduce confidence.

Final Output: {verdict: OVER|UNDER|SKIP, confidence: 0-100, reason: "..."}

docs.title: Cost
docs.text: Each pick evaluation costs ~$0.02 (4 Haiku calls + 1 judge call at $0.80/1M input tokens, ~25 tokens per call).

docs.title: Data Retrieval
docs.text: Uses the nba_api Python package with official NBA Stats API: - Endpoint: https://stats.nba.com/stats - Requires: Player ID (from nba_api's static player registry) - Game logs: 2025-26 season (current)

docs.title: Player Matching
docs.text:
Normalizes player names to handle accents and formatting: 

normalized = unicodedata.normalize("NFD", "Luka Dončić".lower())
normalized = "".join(c for c in normalized if unicodedata.category(c) != "Mn")
# Result: "luka doncic"

Then searches by: 1. Last name first (most likely to be unique) 2. Fallback: First name 3. Filters: Active players only (ignores retired/inactive) 4. Scoring: Best name match from candidates

docs.title: Statistics Tracked
docs.text:
Per-game: PTS, REB, AST, STL, BLK, TOV, FG3M, FGA, FGM, FTA, FTM, MIN

Combo stats auto-calculated: - PTS+REB+AST (fantasy standard) - PTS+AST, PTS+REB, REB+AST (popular flex plays) - STL+BLK (defensive production)

docs.title: Recency Weighting
docs.text:
Recent games weighted more heavily: 

weights = [1.0, 0.95, 0.90, 0.85, 0.80, 0.75, 0.70, 0.65, 0.60, 0.50]
# Game 1 (oldest) → Game 10 (most recent)

docs.title: Timeout Handling
docs.text: nba_api occasionally times out (official API is rate-limited). The module: 1. Retries up to 3 times with exponential backoff 2. Uses 45-second timeout per request 3. Runs in executor (non-blocking async)

docs.title: Data Source
docs.text: api-web.nhle.com — Official NHL Stats API - Free, no authentication, no rate limits - Supports both skaters and goalies - Per-game splits and aggregates

docs.title: Goalie-Specific Stats
docs.text: NHL props frequently feature goalie stats (especially Saves and Saves Against). The module tracks: - Saves (official stat) - Goalies - Saves (PrizePicks naming convention) - Goals Against, Win/Loss

docs.title: Standardization
docs.text: NHL stats names vary between PrizePicks and the official API. The module normalizes: - Points (Skater) → sum of Goals + Assists - Shots On Goal (Skater) - Blocked Shots (Skater) - Saves (Goalie)

docs.title: Data Sources
docs.text:
• VLR.gg Match Data — via vlrdevapi Python package
  • Player search and profile lookups
  • Agent-specific stats (kills, deaths, ACS, rating, KAST per agent)
  • Match schedules (completed and upcoming)

  • Per-map kill counts via HTML scraping

  • PrizePicks Valorant Lines — Partner API with league_id=159

docs.title: Player Lookup
docs.text:
search_player(name: str) returns player profile: 

{
  "player_id": 12345,
  "ign": "starxo",
  "country": "Denmark",
  "team": "Gentle Mates"
}

Caches lookups to avoid repeated searches.

docs.title: Player Averages
docs.text:
get_player_averages(player_id, timespan="90d") calculates: - kills_per_round (KPR) - kills_per_map - avg_acs (Average Combat Score, weighted by rounds played) - avg_rating (VLR performance rating) - avg_kast (Kill, Assist, Survival, Traded percentage) - avg_kd (Kill-to-Death ratio) - Agent breakdown: Usage count and stats per agent (Jett, Sage, Omen, etc.)

30-minute cache to avoid re-scraping VLR within short intervals.

docs.title: Per-Map Kill History
docs.text:
get_player_match_kills(player_id, limit=10) scrapes VLR match pages for: 

{
  "match_id": 12345,
  "map_name": "Abyss",
  "kills": 18,
  "deaths": 5,
  "assists": 2,
  "rating": 1.85,
  "acs": 250,
  "date": "2026-03-28",
  "opponent": "NRG"
}

Parses HTML tables from VLR match detail pages (.vm-stats-game selector).

docs.title: CPU Optimization
docs.text:
VLR scraping can burn 95% CPU if not filtered. The module: - Filters to Valorant-only picks before scraping - Caches results aggressively (30-min TTL on both player averages and match kills) - Limits match history to 10 most recent - Runs BeautifulSoup parsing in a controlled manner

See feedback_vlr_cpu_burn for performance fix applied Mar 2026.

docs.title: Prediction Storage
docs.text:
All picks are stored in sports_predictions table: 

id | player | team | sport | stat_type | direction | line | 
projected | graded_result | verdict | swarm_confidence | 
created_at | game_date | game_id | resolved_at

docs.title: Grading Process
docs.text:
After game resolution: 1. Query final player stats for the day 2. Match each pick to its game result 3. Determine if player exceeded line (OVER) or fell short (UNDER) 4. Update graded_result and resolved_at

Automatic grading runs via: Cron job in cogs/prizepicks_cog.py (daily at 2 AM PST)

docs.title: Accuracy Calculation
docs.text:
get_stats() returns win rate by category: 

{
  "overall": {
    "win_rate": 0.702,      # 70.2% accuracy
    "total": 57,
    "wins": 40,
    "losses": 17
  },
  "by_direction": {
    "OVER": {"wins": 21, "losses": 14, "rate": 0.60},
    "UNDER": {"wins": 19, "losses": 4, "rate": 0.852}
  },
  "by_sport": {
    "NBA": {"wins": 25, "losses": 10, "rate": 0.714},
    "Valorant": {"wins": 5, "losses": 3, "rate": 0.625}
  }
}

docs.title: Edge Case Handling
docs.text:
• Cancelled games: Picks marked as "no_action"
  • Injured players: Flagged pre-game if scrape detects IL status
  • Line movement: Final line at game start used for grading (if available)
  • Prop mismatches: Pick for "Maps 1-2" graded only on those maps (Valorant)

docs.title: Key Files
docs.text: File Purpose modules/sports_props.py Core props fetching, line selection, stat aggregation (NBA, NHL, MLB) modules/sports_swarm.py 4-agent swarm evaluator + judge synthesis (all sports) modules/sports.py Live scores, standings, schedules via ESPN API (secondary system) modules/vlr.py Valorant player lookup, match history, auto-grading against VLR results modules/predictions.py Prediction markets (Polymarket, Kalshi) — separate from sports props cogs/prizepicks_cog.py Discord commands: slip tracking, screenshot parsing, pick history cogs/sports_cog.py Discord commands: live scores, standings, schedules (ESPN) cogs/predictions_cog.py Discord commands: prediction market trends, category views

docs.title: Discord Commands
docs.text:
• /picks report → Daily picks report (top edges, confidence, results)
  • /picks slip → Manual slip entry or parse screenshot
  • /picks history → Win/loss history and accuracy stats
  • /vlr <player> → Valorant player profile + recent stats
  • /scores <league> → Live scores, standings
  • /predictions trending → Trending prediction markets

docs.title: Cron Jobs
docs.text:
• 2 AM PST: Auto-grade daily predictions against finalized game results
  • 3 PM PST: Fetch tomorrow's PrizePicks lines for scheduled games
  • Every 4 hours: Update Valorant match schedule (live picks)

docs.title: Database Tables
docs.text:
• sports_predictions — Graded picks (57+ rows)
  • vlr_match_results — Cached VLR match data
  • prizepicks_slips — User bet tracking (in bets_cog)

docs.title: Current Limitations
docs.text:
• No injury detection (relies on manual flagging)
  • Matchup analyst lacks real-time opponent defensive rankings (uses team reputation)
  • Trends analyst uses only last 10 games (could expand to season-long patterns)
  • Contrarian analyst is rule-based (could use historical trap line detection)

docs.title: Improvements (Roadmap)
docs.text:
• Integrate official injury reports (ESPN or official league APIs)
  • Opponent defensive ranking (by sport: NBA efficiency ratings, NHL Corsi, etc.)
  • Rust-based VLR scraper to replace BeautifulSoup (performance)
  • Machine learning: train model on historical graded picks to weight agents dynamically
  • Under advantage: Model already shows 85.2% accuracy on unders — could weight unders higher

docs.title: Remote Access & Bridge
docs.text:
4 min read · Last updated April 04, 2026 · 745 words

FORGE's mobile access and remote Claude Code integration.

docs.title: Remote Control: Mobile Session Access
docs.text: Spawns claude remote-control CLI for secure remote access from phone/tablet.

docs.title: What It Does
docs.text:
• Runs Claude Code as a plain (non-TUI) process
  • Prints session URL to stdout
  • Stays alive until killed
  • Generates handoff file on stop with git context

docs.title: Starting Remote Control
docs.text:
"start rc"  →  Launches Claude Code remote-control session
             →  Returns session URL for mobile/tablet access

"night mode"  →  Alias for RC (Zack uses at night)

"rc status"  →  Shows current RC session URL, uptime

"stop rc"  →  Kills RC, generates handoff MD with git state

docs.title: Environment Handling
docs.text:
Strips all CLAUDE_*, ANTHROPIC_*, and CLAUDECODE vars before launch. This forces Claude Code to use Max subscription OAuth (not API billing).

Critical vars cleared: - CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN (overrides stored credentials) - CLAUDE_CODE_ENTRYPOINT (breaks RC detection)

docs.title: Handoff Generation
docs.text:
On RC stop, writes to .claude/handoffs/: 

rc_handoff_{timestamp}.md
  ├── git status
  ├── git diff (all changes)
  ├── modified files list
  ├── session metadata (start time, duration)
  └── next-session context

Next local Claude Code session reads this handoff for full context.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: Setting Value Purpose CLAUDE_CMD C:\nvm4w\nodejs\claude.cmd Claude Code CLI path HANDOFF_DIR .claude/handoffs/ Handoff file location _RC_PROCESS subprocess.Popen Managed process handle

docs.title: Mobile Bridge: Claude Code on Your Phone
docs.text: Self-hosted web bridge (Starlette) spawns Claude Code CLI in --print stream-json mode, streams responses to mobile chat UI over WebSocket.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
Phone (WebSocket client)
    ↓
Bridge (Starlette server, port 8080)
    ↓
Claude Code CLI (--print stream-json)
    ↓
max_token_limit response stream

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
Start from Discord: 

"start bridge"  →  Launches on port 8080
                →  Returns: http://your-ip:8080/session-token

"bridge status"  →  Current URL, active sessions

"stop bridge"  →  Shuts down cleanly

Standalone (no Discord): 

python modules/bridge.py --port 8080

docs.title: Features
docs.text:
• Session Manager: tracks concurrent Claude Code sessions (max 10 default)
  • WebSocket streaming: real-time message exchange
  • Auth token: random token per session (URL includes token)
  • Idle cleanup: removes sessions after inactivity
  • Preview text: shows first 100 chars of each session in UI

docs.title: Mobile UI
docs.text: Lightweight HTML5 dashboard: - List active sessions - Create new session - Real-time chat with streaming responses - Message history per session

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
PORT = 8080
MAX_SESSIONS = 10
SESSION_IDLE_TTL = 3600  # 1 hour inactivity

_server_process = None  # managed subprocess
_server_url = None      # broadcast to Discord
_auth_token = None      # random per session

docs.title: Data Classes
docs.text:
SessionInfo: 

internal_id: str       # UUID (first 8 chars)
claude_session_id: Optional[str]
created_at: float
last_active: float
preview: str           # message preview

docs.title: RC Status & Health
docs.text:
Monitor RC/Bridge uptime and activity from Discord.

Command Info rc status RC session URL, uptime, process health bridge status Bridge URL, active sessions, port night mode Start RC for mobile access mobile claude Alias for bridge

docs.title: Security & Auth
docs.text:
• RC: Uses Max OAuth (no API key exposure)
  • Bridge: Random token per session, WebSocket over HTTPS (if deployed)
  • Handoff: Sensitive git state only written to local .claude/handoffs/

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Never share RC/Bridge URLs publicly
  2. RC token is embedded in handoff file—keep .claude/handoffs/ private
  3. Bridge auth token regenerates per session
  4. Kill RC when done (handoff context is one-way, not continuous)

docs.title: Key Files
docs.text: File Purpose modules/remote_control.py RC launcher, process manager, handoff generator modules/bridge.py Bridge server, WebSocket handler, session manager modules/bridge_ui.html Mobile chat UI (embedded in bridge) cogs/remote_control_cog.py Discord command handlers (start, stop, status) cogs/bridge_cog.py Discord bridge handlers .claude/handoffs/ Session context files (git state, changes)

docs.title: Example Workflow
docs.text:
Mobile coding session at night:

Zack (on phone): "start bridge"
FORGE: "Bridge running on http://192.168.1.X:8080/session-token"
       → Zack opens URL on phone → Claude Code chat UI loads
       → "add dark mode toggle to finance dashboard"
       → Claude Code (via bridge) reads code, makes changes, streams response
       → "done, pushed to main"

Next morning (local machine):
FORGE reads .claude/handoffs/bridge_*.md
→ Full context: what was changed, git state, session duration
→ Local Claude Code picks up exactly where mobile left off

See also:

docs.title: Self-Healing and Supervisor Systems
docs.text:
11 min read · Last updated April 04, 2026 · 2,162 words

Last Updated: 2026-04-04 Category: Architecture, Resilience, Operations

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
FORGE implements a three-layer autonomous defense system to achieve "Unkillable FORGE" — a Discord bot that can detect and recover from failures without human intervention. This system bridges the gap between traditional error handling and true autonomy: FORGE detects problems and fixes them independently, rather than waiting for Claude Code to intervene.

The defense comprises:

  1. Preflight Defense (before crash): Import validation catches broken edits before restart
  2. Supervisor Process Management (during runtime): External watchdog manages bot.py lifecycle
  3. Self-Healing Engine (after detection): Autonomous cog recovery, circuit breaker adaptation, and escalation

docs.title: Architecture Diagram
docs.text:
graph TD
    A["forge_supervisor.py<br/>External Process<br/>Launches bot.py<br/>Monitors exit codes<br/>Preflight checks<br/>Crash loop protection<br/>Heartbeat monitoring"] -->|launches/monitors| B["bot.py FORGE Discord"]
    B --> C["Event Bus<br/>independence_loop"]
    C --> D["Learn Loop<br/>Heal Loop<br/>Awareness Loop"]
    D --> E["Watchdog<br/>Silent failure detect"]
    D --> F["Monitor<br/>ERROR+ logging"]
    D --> G["Self-Healer<br/>Escalation<br/>Adaptation<br/>Circuit Breaking"]
    E --> H["📡-monitor<br/>Discord Channel"]
    F --> H
    G --> H

docs.title: Layer 1: Preflight Defense
docs.text: File: forge_supervisor.py (lines 1-100+) Trigger: On FORGE crash (non-zero exit code) Cost: ~2 seconds

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Before restarting bot.py, the supervisor runs import checks on recently modified files. If any file fails to import, it's automatically reverted to the last known-good version.

docs.title: Implementation
docs.text:
# Pseudo-logic from forge_supervisor.py
1. bot.py exits (crash detected)
2. Supervisor scans MODIFIED_FILE_WINDOW (600s = 10 min)
3. For each recently modified Python file:
   - Import file via Python interpreter
   - If ImportError → revert file from git
   - Log reversion to supervisor.log
4. Restart bot.py

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: Parameter Value Purpose MAX_CONSECUTIVE_CRASHES 3 Halt restarts after N fast crashes FAST_CRASH_THRESHOLD 30s Crash within 30s = "fast crash" MODIFIED_FILE_WINDOW 600s Look back 10 minutes for changed files CRASH_COOLDOWN 300s Wait 5 min before retry (after halt)

docs.title: Key Files
docs.text:
• State file: data/supervisor_state.json — JSON record of last crash, reverted files, state transitions
  • PID file: data/supervisor_pid.json — Supervisor process ID (for external monitoring)
  • Log: logs/supervisor.log — All supervisor actions, formatted with timestamps

docs.title: Example Scenario
docs.text:
10:15:03 [FORGE.supervisor] Starting bot.py (PID 8492)
10:15:35 [FORGE.supervisor] bot.py crashed with code 1
10:15:35 [FORGE.supervisor] Scanning modified files (last 600s)
10:15:35 [FORGE.supervisor] Found: cogs/my_cog.py (modified 2min ago)
10:15:36 [FORGE.supervisor] Preflight check: import cogs.my_cog → ImportError
10:15:36 [FORGE.supervisor] Reverting cogs/my_cog.py to last git commit
10:15:37 [FORGE.supervisor] Starting bot.py (attempt 1/3)

docs.title: Limitations
docs.text:
• Only catches import-time errors (syntax, missing dependencies, circular imports)
  • Does NOT catch runtime errors (those are handled by Layer 3)
  • Cannot revert files if git is unavailable or repo is corrupted

docs.title: Layer 2: Supervisor Process Management
docs.text: File: forge_supervisor.py (lines 40-300+) Lifecycle: Continuous during FORGE runtime

docs.title: Core Responsibilities
docs.text:
1. Subprocess Management — Launches bot.py with proper I/O handling
  2. Heartbeat Monitoring — Checks data/heartbeat.json written by watchdog every 10 minutes
  3. Crash Loop Protection — Halts after MAX_CONSECUTIVE_CRASHES fast crashes
  4. Recorder Co-Management — Also launches/monitors forge_recorder.py (standalone voice recorder)
  5. State Tracking — Writes to supervisor_state.json for visibility

docs.title: Heartbeat System
docs.text:
The supervisor reads a heartbeat file written by the watchdog module (every 10 minutes during active hours).

{
  "timestamp": "2026-04-04T15:30:00Z",
  "uptime_seconds": 3600,
  "memory_mb": 425.3,
  "event_loop_lag": 0.002,
  "last_message": "2026-04-04T15:29:45Z"
}

If the heartbeat is stale for > 20 minutes (HEARTBEAT_STALE_THRESHOLD), the supervisor considers FORGE hung and may restart it.

docs.title: State Transitions
docs.text:
graph TD
    A["HEALTHY"] -->|crash detected| B["CRASHED<br/>Preflight check<br/>Revert files"]
    B -->|fast restart succeeds| A
    B -->|fast restart fails| C["CRASHED again<br/>Repeat until<br/>MAX_CRASHES"]
    C -->|MAX_CRASHES reached| D["HALTED"]
    D -->|Wait CRASH_COOLDOWN 300s| E["Reset counters"]
    E --> A

docs.title: Recorder Co-Management
docs.text:
The supervisor also manages forge_recorder.py, a standalone process that: - Captures FORGE's output in a ring buffer - Survives bot crashes (separate process) - Written to in real-time by FORGE - Readable for diagnostic purposes

Launched alongside bot.py, managed with same lifecycle.

docs.title: Layer 3: Self-Healing Engine
docs.text:
Files: - core/self_healing.py — Main SelfHealer class - core/resilience.py — Circuit breaker pattern - core/error_recovery.py — Discord error recovery buttons - modules/forge_watchdog.py — Silent failure detection - modules/forge_monitor.py — Centralized error logging - modules/circuit_breaker.py — DB-backed circuit breaker state - core/forge_loop.py — Event bus wiring

Trigger: Autonomous, scheduled + event-driven Scope: Self-correcting without human input

docs.title: 3A. Cog Auto-Recovery
docs.text:
When a cog (module) crashes or becomes unresponsive:

# From self_healing.py :: CogAutoRecovery
1. Watchdog detects cog not responding to events
2. Triggers self_healer.recover_cog(cog_name)
3. Unloads broken cog with await bot.unload_extension()
4. Waits 2 seconds (resource cleanup)
5. Reloads cog with await bot.load_extension()
6. Posts recovery message to #📡-monitor

Status tracking: Cog-level recovery tracked in database, history in watchdog_cog_recovery table.

docs.title: 3B. Failure Adaptation (Circuit Breaker)
docs.text:
File: core/resilience.py Pattern: Three-state finite state machine

The circuit breaker wraps external API calls (Anthropic, GitHub, Apify, Tavily, Exa) with failure tracking:

graph TD
    A["CLOSED<br/>Normal"] -->|N consecutive failures| B["OPEN<br/>Rejecting calls"]
    B -->|timeout elapsed| C["HALF_OPEN<br/>Testing recovery"]
    C -->|M successes| A
    C -->|any failure| B

Configuration (per-service, in CircuitConfig):

Parameter Default Meaning failure_threshold 5 Open after N failures success_threshold 2 Close after N successes in HALF_OPEN reset_timeout 60s Wait before trying HALF_OPEN half_open_max_calls 3 Max concurrent test calls

Database-backed via modules/circuit_breaker.py:

CREATE TABLE circuit_breakers (
  service TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
  state TEXT,        -- 'closed', 'open', 'half_open'
  failure_count INT,
  opened_at TEXT,    -- ISO8601
  cooldown_seconds INT
)

Automatic Adaptation: The self-healer periodically reviews failure patterns and adjusts thresholds. High-failure services get stricter thresholds; recovering services get relaxed thresholds.

docs.title: 3C. Friction Escalation
docs.text:
File: core/self_healing.py :: FrictionEscalator

Detects recurring error patterns and promotes them to enforced preferences:

# Escalation workflow
1. Every 6 hours, scan friction.jsonl (error history)
2. Count patterns: "API timeout on enrichment" (3x in past week)
3. If pattern exceeds threshold:
   a. Create preference rule: "Retry enrichment API max 2 times, then skip"
   b. Write to preference_rules table
   c. Reload classifier to bake in rule
   d. Post escalation to #📡-monitor: "🔥 Escalated: Enrichment API retry limit"

Benefits: - Learns from repeated failures - Prevents cascading failures by making recovery automatic - Reduces human intervention (no need to re-architect after pattern emerges)

docs.title: 3D. Health Pulse
docs.text:
Schedule: Every 15 minutes Scope: Periodic self-check

Runs comprehensive health checks: - Event loop lag (should be < 0.01s) - Memory usage (alert if > 80% of baseline) - Database connections (all open?) - Scheduled tasks (all running on time?) - Discord connectivity (guild members visible?) - Last message timestamp (recent or stale?)

Posts summary to #📡-monitor with color-coded status: - 🟢 All healthy - 🟡 Warnings (e.g., lag > 0.005s but < 0.02s) - 🔴 Critical (e.g., event loop lag > 0.05s)

docs.title: Watchdog Module
docs.text:
File: modules/forge_watchdog.py

Goes beyond crash monitoring — detects silent failures:

  • Scheduled tasks not firing (briefing, email, meeting prep, autopilot)
  • Commands going unanswered
  • Stale data (weather, emails, last message timestamp)
  • API degradation (slow responses, auth failures)
  • Resource issues (memory growth, event loop lag)

Schedule: Every 10 minutes during active hours (7am-11pm) Cost: $0 (pure Python introspection, no API calls) Alert States:

Severity Color Meaning 🟢 Recovery Green Was broken, now working (auto-clears after 2 consecutive passes) 🟡 Warning Yellow Degraded but functional 🔴 Critical Red Something is broken

Alert state machine prevents spam: once alerted, won't re-alert same issue for 30 minutes (cooldown).

docs.title: Monitor Module
docs.text:
File: modules/forge_monitor.py

Hooks into Python's logging system to catch all ERROR+ level events:

  1. DiscordErrorHandler — Logging handler that captures logger.error() and logger.critical()
  2. Deduplication — Same error suppressed for 5 minutes (prevents spam)
  3. Rate Limiting — Queues Discord posts, processes sequentially
  4. Module Tracking — Counts errors per module, alerts if spike detected
  5. Startup Health Check — Posts boot summary to #📡-monitor on bot ready

Every error becomes a Discord embed in #📡-monitor:

🔴 ERROR: enrichment_api
Module: FORGE.modules.enrichment
Func: enrich_candidate_sync
Message: "API timeout after 30s"
Time: 2026-04-04 15:42:19 UTC

docs.title: Process Monitor
docs.text:
File: modules/process_monitor.py

Wires into the process registry singleton and posts lifecycle events to #📡-monitor:

  • ▶️ Started — Background task launched
  • ✅ Completed — Task finished successfully (includes duration)
  • ❌ Failed — Task crashed (includes error)
  • 🛑 Stopped — Task manually stopped

Example embed: 

▶️ Started: enrichment_batch
Channel: #⚡-forge
Source: scheduler (hourly job)
Expected: 15 mins

docs.title: Independence Loop (Event Bus Wiring)
docs.text:
File: core/forge_loop.py

Connects all feedback loops via event bus subscriptions (fire-and-forget):

docs.title: Learn Loop
docs.text:
graph TD
    A["Classifier corrected"] --> B["Update WAL"]
    B --> C["Pattern detection"]
    C --> D["Dynamic few-shot"]
    D --> E["Better classifier"]

When Zack corrects the classifier, the correction is logged to the Write-Ahead Log (WAL), then used to improve the classifier's few-shot examples.

docs.title: Heal Loop
docs.text:
graph TD
    A["API failure detected"] --> B["Circuit breaker trips"]
    B --> C["Self-healer notified"]
    C --> D["Threshold adaptation"]
    D --> E["Monitor alert posted"]

docs.title: Awareness Loop
docs.text:
graph TD
    A["Unknown request"] --> B["Gap recorded"]
    B --> C["Brain analyzes"]
    C --> D["Suggests exact command"]

When the classifier returns None (no match), the user's message is recorded as a capability gap, and the brain (Sonnet) later suggests which command to add or which classifier rule to fix.

docs.title: Error Recovery with User Choice
docs.text:
File: core/error_recovery.py

When an error occurs during a user-facing operation (enrichment, scraping, etc.), the bot:

  1. Detects error type via regex pattern matching
  2. Suggests recovery actions with Discord buttons
  3. Waits for user choice (retry, skip, switch provider, check logs)
  4. Acts on choice and continues operation

Example:

⚠️ Enrichment API timeout after 30s
[🔄 Retry] [⏳ Wait 60s] [⏭️ Skip]

Recovery patterns recognized:

  • Timeouts → Retry / Skip
  • Rate limits → Wait + Retry / Skip
  • Connection errors → Retry / Skip
  • Auth errors → Check Logs / Skip
  • Database errors → Retry / Check Logs
  • Circuit breaker open → Wait + Retry / Skip

docs.title: Key Files Reference Table
docs.text: File Purpose Lines Key Function forge_supervisor.py External watchdog, auto-revert, crash loop protection ~400 Main supervisor loop, preflight checks core/self_healing.py Main self-healer, orchestrates all recovery ~300 SelfHealer.init(), run_friction_escalation() core/resilience.py Circuit breaker pattern, async wrapper ~400 AsyncCircuitBreaker, BreakerRegistry core/error_recovery.py Discord error buttons, user-driven recovery ~200 ErrorRecoveryView, suggest_recovery() modules/circuit_breaker.py DB-backed circuit breaker state ~150 check_breaker(), record_success() modules/forge_watchdog.py Silent failure detection, 10-min checks ~500+ run_all_checks(), init_watchdog() modules/forge_monitor.py Centralized error logging to Discord ~400+ DiscordErrorHandler, setup_logging() modules/process_monitor.py Process lifecycle events to #📡-monitor ~150 _handle_event() core/forge_loop.py Event bus wiring for feedback loops ~150 setup_independence_loop()

docs.title: When to Trust Auto-Recovery
docs.text:
• Simple cog reloads (network glitch, timeout)
  • API circuit breaker trips (service degraded)
  • Friction escalation (pattern-based optimization)
  • Watchdog alerts (informational, no action taken)

docs.title: When to Intervene Manually
docs.text:
• Multiple consecutive crashes (supervisor halts after 3)
  • Heartbeat stale for 20+ minutes (possible infinite loop)
  • High error rate spike (> 10 errors/hour in single module)
  • Database corruption or git repo issues

docs.title: Debugging Commands
docs.text:
# Check supervisor state
cat data/supervisor_state.json | jq .

# View supervisor logs (real-time)
tail -f logs/supervisor.log

# Check heartbeat freshness
cat data/heartbeat.json | jq .timestamp

# Check circuit breaker states
sqlite3 data/forge.db "SELECT * FROM circuit_breakers"

# Check watchdog alerts (DB)
sqlite3 data/forge.db "SELECT * FROM watchdog_alerts ORDER BY alerted_at DESC LIMIT 20"

# Manually trigger health check
discord command: "watchdog check"

# View monitor channel logs
discord: #📡-monitor (scroll up to see all alerts)

docs.title: Configuration for Custom Behavior
docs.text:
Edit these constants in source files:

Supervisor (forge_supervisor.py): 

MAX_CONSECUTIVE_CRASHES = 3          # Halt after N crashes
FAST_CRASH_THRESHOLD = 30            # Crash within 30s = fast crash
CRASH_COOLDOWN = 300                 # 5 min wait before retry
HEARTBEAT_STALE_THRESHOLD = 1200     # 20 min = consider hung

Self-Healer (core/self_healing.py): 

# Friction escalation every 6 hours
# Failure adaptation every 30 minutes
# Health pulse every 15 minutes

Watchdog (modules/forge_watchdog.py): 

_CHECK_INTERVAL = 10                 # 10 min between checks
_ALERT_COOLDOWN = 30 * 60            # 30 min before re-alert
_RECOVERY_THRESHOLD = 2              # 2 consecutive passes to clear

docs.title: FORGE Sourcing and Outreach Pipeline
docs.text: 13 min read · Last updated April 04, 2026 · 2,680 words

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
The FORGE sourcing and outreach pipeline is a multi-stage, AI-driven system that discovers engineering candidates from external sources (GitHub, Slack communities, company raids), enriches their LinkedIn profiles, scores them using PRISM evaluation, and generates personalized outreach messages. The pipeline is orchestrated via Sourcing Pipeline (4-agent orchestrator) and can run on-demand or automatically overnight via Overnight Engine.

Core flow: 

[Strategist] → [Scout] → [Enricher] → [Evaluator] → [Outreach] → [Ashby/Tracking]
   (Plan)     (Discover)  (Enrich)    (Score 1-5)  (Personalize) (Pipeline)

Each stage is independently parallelizable, allowing the pipeline to scale across multiple concurrent discovery streams (GitHub, Slack communities, company raids) while maintaining cost control and quality gates.

docs.title: Company Raids
docs.text:
Company Raider executes a two-phase discovery targeting engineers at specific companies:

Stage 1: Apify Batch Search - Query LinkedIn by company name and experience bracket (typically 2-7yr and 6-10yr) - CRITICAL: Apify calls are always batched — never one profile at a time (prevents $200+ waste on setup fees) - Batch size: 200 profiles per flush - Cost: ~$0.003/profile via Apify batch API

Stage 2: Post-Filter & Dedup - Filter by job title (ENGINEER_KEYWORDS: "engineer", "ml", "ai", "backend", etc.) - Remove executive roles (VP, director, founder) - Geographic filter: Bay Area focus (SF, Palo Alto, Mountain View, etc.) - Experience validation: 2-7yr junior roles flagged as "senior_backlog" if 8+yr - Dedup against: - Ashby pipeline (candidates already in ATS) - candidate_evals table (already scored) - profile_scrape_cache (already scraped today)

Output: - Qualified candidates forwarded to PRISM scorer - Senior overqualified candidates tagged for future hiring rounds

Data files: - Target companies: data/ai_companies_master.csv - Bay Area city list: hardcoded in raider - Blocklist: COMPANY_BLOCKLIST (Google, Meta, Amazon, etc.)

docs.title: LinkedIn Enrichment
docs.text: LinkedIn profile enrichment happens in two flavors:

docs.title: RapidAPI (Primary, Lower Cost)
docs.text:
linkedin_ops.py integrates with fresh-linkedin-profile-data.p.rapidapi.com: - Pulls public profile data (education, experience, skills) in JSON - Cost: ~$0.006/profile (lower than Apify, but less detailed) - Used for quick screening when full Apify scrape not justified - Batch requests allowed — parallelize up to 10 concurrent

Never use Apify for LinkedIn. (see MEMORY rules) — prior usage wasted $200+ on per-call overhead.

docs.title: Apify Batch Scraping (Fallback)
docs.text:
pipeline_enricher.py uses supreme_coder~linkedin-profile-scraper actor: - Full LinkedIn profile extraction: headline, experience, education, skills, endorsements, recommendations - 200 profiles per batch to amortize setup cost (~$5 overhead per run) - Timeout: 5 minutes before batch flushes - Cost: ~$0.003/profile (cheaper per-unit than RapidAPI, but requires batching)

Pre-filtering via Haiku: - Before Apify batch, all URLs get Haiku pre-filter (JD-relevance check) - Helps drop obvious non-matches before expensive scraping - Improvement: 30-40% of discovered URLs filtered before Apify

Post-filtering: - Full profiles checked against sourcing_filters (years exp, location, company) - Failing profiles dropped before scoring

Storage: - Raw profiles stored in pipeline_profiles table - Cached indefinitely; dedup prevents re-scraping same URL within cooldown window

docs.title: PRISM Scoring
docs.text:
pipeline_evaluator.py runs PRISM scoring on enriched profiles using Claude Sonnet.

Score Scale (1-5): - 5: Strong match — multiple must-haves met, relevant tech stack, seniority aligned, geographic fit - 4: Good match — most must-haves, solid background, some gaps okay (nice-to-haves) - 3: Possible match — core requirements met, but questions around depth/breadth - 2: Weak match — missing 2-3 key requirements, requires significant backfill - 1: Not a fit — fundamentally misaligned (e.g., frontend-only for backend role)

Input: Enriched LinkedIn profile + JD content (title, requirements, seniority, tech stack)

Output: Scored record with: - Score (1-5) - Reasoning (why this score) - Timing signal (hire urgently vs. backlog vs. not now) - Outreach context (what to emphasize)

Batch processing: 10 profiles at a time to maximize Sonnet throughput, minimize latency overhead

Recent layoff flagging: Candidates from Meta, Amazon, Google, Microsoft, Salesforce, etc. flagged as potentially motivated (recent layoff → active job search)

Database: Stored in candidate_evals table with full PRISM report

docs.title: Outreach Generation
docs.text:
linkedin_ops.py and Ashby ATS feed outreach generation.

Quality Gate: 25-Rule Evaluator All outreach runs through rules check before showing to Zack:

Hard Bans (Fix before posting): - NO em dashes (—) anywhere. Use commas, periods, parentheses instead. - NO "Worth a chat/conversation/call/anything" — banned phrase - NO "on your radar" — overused - NO "I came across your profile" or "I was impressed by" - NO filler ("That's a unique combo", "That's exactly the kind...") - NO restating candidate's resume back to them - NO time commitments ("15 min", "quick call") - NO claims about HockeyStack unless verified by Arda - NO claim of contractor status without verification

Format Rules: - 1-2 sentences MAX - :) only closes observation sentence, never mid-sentence - Blurb only — no "Hi [Name]" or "Arda" wrapper (Ashby template handles that) - NO CTA as final line — personalization is the ask. Observation closes it. - Sample CTAs: "Happy to share more if you're curious." / "Open to chatting if you are." / Ask a genuine question instead.

Style (Arda's Voice): - Casual, direct, no corporate fluff - Show you read their profile — reference specific project or skill - Personal observation at end: "Your [specific thing] stands out for [why it matters]" - Never compare to other candidates - One at a time — never bulk templates

Example (good):

Your work on distributed systems at Stripe + move to Cursor seems like you're betting big on AI-first tooling. That's exactly the vibe we're building at HockeyStack :)

Example (bad):

Hey John, I came across your profile and was impressed by your background. I think we have an interesting opportunity that might be worth a conversation. Let me know if you're open to a quick 15-min call!

docs.title: Sourcing Pipeline Orchestration
docs.text: sourcing_pipeline.py coordinates the 4-agent pipeline:

docs.title: Agent 1: Strategist
docs.text:
Runs once per pipeline execution. Takes JD + config, produces SourcingPlan:

  • JD Analysis (Haiku): Extract must-haves, nice-to-haves, deal-breakers, seniority, location preferences
  • Company Research (Exa): Discover competitors, adjacent markets, funding announcements
  • GitHub Repo Discovery: Find popular repos in target tech stack (e.g., if JD is Python ML, discover popular ML repos)
  • Stream Configuration: Craft per-stream search queries:
  • GitHub: Search repos by language + topic tags + contributor filtering
  • Slack: Target LangChain, MLOps, Convergence communities
  • Company: List of target companies from replenisher (via ai_companies_master.csv)
  • Budget Allocation: Equal split by default (33% each for 3 streams)
  • Cost Estimation: Extrapolate to max_profiles, give Zack approval dialog if >$100

Output: SourcingPlan dict consumed by Scout, Enricher, Evaluator

docs.title: Agent 2: Scout
docs.text:
Runs 3 parallel streams (GitHub, Slack, Company), pushes LinkedIn URLs to url_queue.

GitHub Stream: - For each repo in plan: query GitHub API for contributors - Extract real names, compute activity scores - Search LinkedIn for each name + "GitHub" + "engineering" - Push matches to url_queue

Slack Stream: - For each Slack workspace (LangChain, MLOps, Convergence): read member list - Extract Slack profile → name + bio - Search LinkedIn for name + bio keywords - Push matches to url_queue

Company Stream: - For each company in plan: use Company Raider to execute Apify batch search - Filter per post-filter rules - Push LinkedIn URLs to url_queue

Dedup & Cooldown: - Skip sources scraped within 7 days (cooldown window) - Skip duplicates (normalize LinkedIn URLs before pushing) - Log scrape in pipeline_scrape_log table

Sentinel Signal: - When all 3 streams complete, Scout pushes _SENTINEL to url_queue - Signals Enricher that discovery phase is done

docs.title: Agent 3: Enricher
docs.text:
Drains url_queue, filters, enriches, post-filters, pushes to profile_queue.

Pre-filter (Haiku): - For each URL batch: call Haiku with JD summary + LinkedIn URL - Haiku predicts "relevant" or "not relevant" - Drop non-relevant URLs before expensive Apify scrape (saves 30-40% Apify cost)

Batch Scraping: - Accumulate URLs into batch of 200 (or 5 min timeout) - Call Apify batch scraper via actor supreme_coder~linkedin-profile-scraper - Parallel profile extraction: company, education, skills, experience - Cost: ~$0.003/profile (Apify cost gate: if batch >100, ask Zack before proceeding)

Post-filter: - Run sourcing_filters.post_filter_profiles() on raw profiles - Check: years experience, location, company, seniority, title keywords - Drop failing profiles

Output: - Passing profiles pushed to profile_queue - Stats updated: enriched count, Apify cost, pass/fail counts - Sentinel forwarded when url_queue exhausted

docs.title: Agent 4: Evaluator
docs.text:
Drains profile_queue, PRISM-scores with Sonnet, generates outreach, saves to DB, posts to Discord.

Batch Scoring (10 at a time): - Accumulate profiles from queue into batch - Call Sonnet with PRISM evaluator prompt - Return score + reasoning for each

Timing Signals: - Low score (1-2): "backlog" — file for future rounds - Medium score (3): "maybe" — needs follow-up research - High score (4-5): "pursue now" — generate outreach immediately

Outreach Context: - Extract key differentiators (tech stack match, seniority fit, recent company) - Suggest opening angle (what to emphasize in message) - Flag potential concerns (gap in background, geographic concern)

Database Save: - pipeline_results table: candidate, score, reasoning, timing, session_id - candidate_evals table: full PRISM report for later reference - sourced_profiles table: dedup against auto_sourcing engine

Discord Post: - Summary embed per batch: count scored, distribution (5s, 4s, 3s, 2s, 1s) - Top candidates listed with score + opening angle - Post to channel (if configured) - DM Zack with final summary + cost breakdown

docs.title: Ashby Integration
docs.text: FORGE integrates with Ashby ATS via two methods:

docs.title: API Integration
docs.text:
ashby.py uses Ashby REST API (no SDK) for: - List jobs (open, archived, all) - List candidates (applications) - Get interview schedules - Update candidate status - Archive candidates

Auth: Basic auth (API key as username, blank password) All endpoints: POST to https://api.ashbyhq.com/{endpoint}

CRITICAL: Ashby has NO public SDK and NO JavaScript export. The Ashby API is read-only for candidate listing — no bulk import capability. This drives the browser automation approach for pipeline scraping.

docs.title: Browser-Based Dashboard Scraping
docs.text:
ashby_dashboard.py uses forge-browser MCP to scrape Ashby UI:

Sequence Templates Dashboard: - Navigate to https://app.ashbyhq.com/sourcing/sequence-templates - Extract: sequence name, enrolled count, reply rate, conversion rate, created_by, last_modified - Used for real-time pipeline visibility + outreach performance tracking

Candidate Detail: - Open individual sequence - Extract applied candidates, interview stages, outcomes - Used for "which candidates are in which stage" queries

Why browser automation? Ashby's API doesn't expose sequences or email campaign data. Scraping the UI is the only way to pull live pipeline metrics.

docs.title: Overnight Engine
docs.text:
overnight_engine.py runs autonomous deep work during VPS off-peak hours.

Schedule: 4 cycles per night - 9:30 PM Pacific (5:30 UTC) - 12:30 AM (8:30 UTC) - 3:30 AM (11:30 UTC) - 6:30 AM (2:30 UTC)

Cost Cap: $10/night (actual spend typically $3-6)

Task Prioritization: 1. Continue yesterday's sourcing pipeline (enrich, score, post outreach) 2. Deep candidate research (GitHub activity, blog posts, open source) 3. Company intelligence (build profiles for target companies) 4. Stale outreach follow-ups (identify candidates who need nudging) 5. Knowledge base maintenance (re-index docs, prune duplicates)

Per-Cycle Execution: - Pick highest-priority task - Run for up to 30 minutes - Log results + cost to overnight_state.json - Post summary to ⚡-forge channel

Example overnight workflow: - 9:30 PM: Flush partial enrichment queue, score remaining profiles - 12:30 AM: Deep dive on top 10 candidates (GitHub activity, tweets, blog) - 3:30 AM: Replenish company list (mine previous candidates' employers, web discovery) - 6:30 AM: Post morning briefing with new candidates, overnight spend summary

docs.title: Auto-Sourcing Engine
docs.text:
auto_sourcing.py runs autonomous sourcing 2x/day on VPS.

Per-cycle pipeline: 1. Haiku generates sourcing niche (e.g., "Kafka engineers at Series B data startups") 2. Serper finds company domains in that niche 3. Apollo searches those domains for engineers 4. Dedup filter: skip URLs in sourced_profiles table 5. Apify scrapes only NEW profiles 6. PRISM scores 1-5 7. Post 4+ candidates to 🎯-talent channel, DM Zack summary

Budget Cap: 2,000 Apify profiles/day. If hit → DM Zack for approval before proceeding.

Configuration: - Title allowlist: software engineer, ML engineer, AI engineer, backend, data engineer, platform engineer - Seniority: senior, manager, entry - Title blocklist: founder, CEO, VP, director - JD: backend_engineer_ai_focused.txt

Output: - Discord post to 🎯-talent (high-score candidates) - DM to Zack with summary (how many scored, cost spent, highlights) - Database: sourced_profiles table (LinkedIn URL → score → timestamp)

docs.title: ENG Sync Agenda Generation
docs.text:
meeting_agenda.py auto-generates Friday 1:1 agenda for Zack + Hank meeting.

Inputs: - Agenda template (templates/friday_meeting_agenda.md) - Open action items (meeting_action_items.json) — filtered to Zack/Hank/Unassigned - Recent FORGE changelog (tooling updates from past 7 days) - Previous meeting notes (analysis + unresolved items) - Ashby pipeline metrics (via ashby_dashboard: sequences, conversion rates, candidate stages)

Output: - Pre-populated Friday agenda in Markdown - Action items grouped by owner - Overdue items flagged - Recent tooling updates listed - Candidate pipeline summary (stage breakdown, conversion funnel)

Usage: "generate friday agenda" / "prep friday meeting" via Discord → generates agenda file, uploads to ⚡-forge channel

docs.title: Company Replenisher
docs.text:
company_replenisher.py auto-discovers new companies to raid.

Strategy 1: Mine Existing Candidates - Query candidate_evals where score >= 3.0 - Extract previous employers from profile experience - Example: High-scorer from Cursor who previously worked at "Acme AI" → add "Acme AI" to raid list - Theory: If we scored them high, their previous companies are also worth raiding

Strategy 2: Web Discovery - Haiku generates "trending AI startups + recent funding rounds" - Serper searches for companies matching keywords - Validate company exists + has engineering team - Add to ai_companies_master.csv

Output: - ai_companies_master.csv (master list of target companies) - Feeds into Overnight Engine Company Raids task - Next cycle raids new companies discovered

docs.title: Sourcing Tracker
docs.text:
sourcing_tracker.py tracks sourced candidates and target companies via Discord.

Candidate Tracking: - "s+ John Doe dba" → quick add (shorthand: s+ = source add) - Bulk add with role suffix parsing (dba, lead, support, relo) - Embed scoreboard with progress bars + streak tracking

Company Pipeline: - Target count vs. sourced count - Conversion rate (sourced/target) - List active targets

Features: - Quick add (shorthand syntax for speed) - Undo history (revert last batch add) - Display number → DB ID mapping (click "2" in list, get right record) - Export to text (HireIQ format for sync)

docs.title: Key Files

docs.title: Text-to-Speech & Voice System
docs.text:
9 min read · Last updated April 04, 2026 · 1,845 words

FORGE integrates ElevenLabs AI voice synthesis for reading text, messages, URLs, and morning briefs aloud. The system features intelligent voice rotation that ensures you never hear the same voice twice in a row, with audio files stored locally for caching and replay.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The TTS system consists of three layers: 1. API layer (modules/tts.py) — ElevenLabs integration, voice rotation logic, MP3 generation 2. Action handlers (cogs/tts_actions.py) — Discord command dispatch for read text/last/URL/brief 3. Trigger intercept (bot.py on_message) — Fast-path recognition of "read this" phrases before classifier

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
Message → on_message TTS intercept
         ↓
    "read this ..." → tts_read_text
    "read that" → tts_read_last
    "read brief" → tts_read_brief
         ↓
    classifier (if no TTS intercept match)
         ↓
    brain / cog dispatch

The TTS intercept is positioned at the top of on_message (before classifier) to provide sub-100ms response for common voice commands.

docs.title: ElevenLabs Integration
docs.text: Provider: ElevenLabs API v1 Base URL: https://api.elevenlabs.io/v1 Auth: ELEVENLABS_API_KEY in .env Model: eleven_turbo_v2_5 (fast + cost-efficient, default) Quality model: eleven_multilingual_v2 (highest quality, 29 languages)

docs.title: API Flow
docs.text:
POST /v1/text-to-speech/{voice_id}
Headers:
  - xi-api-key: <ELEVENLABS_API_KEY>
  - Content-Type: application/json
Body:
  {
    "text": "...",
    "model_id": "eleven_turbo_v2_5",
    "voice_settings": {
      "stability": 0.5,      # 0 = expressive, 1 = monotone
      "similarity_boost": 0.75
    }
  }

Response:
  200 OK → audio/mpeg binary stream (MP3)
  != 200 → JSON error message

docs.title: Never Repeat Last Voice
docs.text:
The voice selection logic guarantees you never hear the same voice twice in a row. This is critical for morning briefs, daily reads, and repeated use cases.

Algorithm (in pick_daily_voice()):

  1. Load voice usage history from data/tts/voice_history.json
  2. Filter available voices: exclude used list AND last_voice
  3. If all voices exhausted: reset used list (but still exclude last_voice)
  4. Pick random voice from filtered pool
  5. Append voice_id to used list and save history
  6. Log: "Today's voice: {name} ({accent}, {gender})"

docs.title: Voice History Schema
docs.text:
{
  "used": ["voice_id_1", "voice_id_2", "..."],
  "last_voice": "voice_id_3",
  "last_date": "2026-04-04"
}

Storage: data/tts/voice_history.json

docs.title: Voice Metadata
docs.text: Each voice from GET /v1/voices includes: - voice_id: Unique identifier (used in TTS requests) - name: Display name (e.g., "Adam", "Alice", "Luna") - preview_url: Sample audio clip - labels: Object with accent (e.g., "American"), gender (e.g., "male")

docs.title: Text-to-Speech API
docs.text: Module: modules/tts.py

docs.title: Main Function: text_to_speech()
docs.text:
async def text_to_speech(
    text: str,
    output_path: str | Path | None = None,
    voice_id: str | None = None,
    model: str = DEFAULT_MODEL,
    stability: float = 0.5,
    similarity_boost: float = 0.75,
) -> Path:
    """Convert text to speech using ElevenLabs.

    Args:
        text: Text to speak
        output_path: Where to save MP3. Auto-generated if None.
        voice_id: Specific voice to use. If None, picks today's rotation voice.
        model: ElevenLabs model ID
        stability: Voice stability (0-1). Lower = more expressive.
        similarity_boost: How closely to match the voice (0-1).

    Returns:
        Path to generated MP3 file.
    """

Behavior: - If voice_id is None: calls pick_daily_voice() to get rotation voice - Generates timestamped filename: brief_20260404_145230.mp3 - Saves to data/tts/ directory - Logs file size (KB), character count, and voice name - Raises ValueError if API fails or no API key configured

Example Usage:

# Auto voice rotation
audio_path = await text_to_speech("Hello, this is a test.")
# Returns: Path("data/tts/brief_20260404_145230.mp3")

# Specific voice
audio_path = await text_to_speech(
    "Hello, this is a test.",
    voice_id="adam",  # Use "Adam" voice specifically
    stability=0.3,     # More expressive
)

docs.title: Helper Functions
docs.text: Function Purpose _get_api_key() Loads ELEVENLABS_API_KEY from .env or environment _load_voice_history() Reads voice_history.json, returns dict with used/last_voice _save_voice_history(history) Writes voice history to JSON get_available_voices() Fetches full voice list from ElevenLabs API pick_daily_voice(voices) Selects next voice in rotation, never repeats last speak_alert(text, channel) Generate + post audio alert to Discord channel morning_brief_audio(brief_text) Render morning brief as audio (see morning-brief)

docs.title: Action Handlers
docs.text:
Module: cogs/tts_actions.py Cog: TTSCog

Dispatched by classifier when TTS actions detected. Each handler runs async, posts status/result to Discord.

docs.title: tts_read_text — Read Arbitrary Text
docs.text:
Trigger: "read this text", "say this message", "read aloud: ..." Handler: _read_text(intent, message)

  1. Extract text from message or intent.query
  2. Try prefixes: "read this:", "say this:", "read aloud:", etc.
  3. Call text_to_speech(text)
  4. If MP3 < 10 MB: post as Discord audio file
  5. If >= 10 MB: post error message suggesting shorter text

Prefixes Recognized: - "read this out loud:" / "read this out loud" - "read this:" / "read this" - "say this:" / "say this" - "read aloud:" / "read aloud" - "speak:" / "speak"

docs.title: tts_read_last — Read Previous Message
docs.text:
Trigger: "read that", "read the last message", "read that out loud" Handler: _read_last(message)

  1. Search channel history (limit 5) for previous non-bot message
  2. Fallback to bot messages if no human message found
  3. Call text_to_speech(previous_message.content)
  4. Post MP3 to channel

Error Handling: Returns "No previous message to read." if history is empty.

docs.title: tts_read_url — Scrape, Summarize, Read URL
docs.text:
Trigger: "read this URL", "read this link" Handler: _read_url(intent, message)

  1. Extract URL from intent.query or message content (regex)
  2. Fetch page via aiohttp (timeout: 15s)
  3. Strip HTML tags with regex: <[^>]+> → space
  4. Collapse whitespace
  5. Truncate to first 3000 chars (avoid ElevenLabs char limits)
  6. Call text_to_speech(text)
  7. Post MP3 to channel

Error Handling: - URL fetch timeout or non-200 status → "Couldn't fetch URL (status X)" - TTS API error → propagated to user

docs.title: tts_read_brief — Morning Brief Audio
docs.text:
Trigger: "read brief", "read the brief", "listen to brief" Handler: _read_brief(message)

  1. Check for local file: data/tts/latest_brief.md
  2. If not found: search channel history (limit 20) for "Morning Brief" or "Dream Cycle" text
  3. Call morning_brief_audio(brief_text) to render audio
  4. Post MP3 with caption "Morning Brief — audio:"

Related: See morning-brief for brief generation pipeline.

docs.title: TTS Intercept (Fast-Path)
docs.text:
Location: bot.py on_message() — positioned before classifier

Trigger Recognition:

# Normalize punctuation after trigger phrase
_tts_normalized = re.sub(
    r"^(read this|say this|speak this|forge read|forge say|tts|read aloud)[.,;:!?\s]+",
    r"\1 ",
    message.content.lower()
)

# Check if starts with normalized trigger
if _tts_normalized.startswith(("read this ", "say this ", "tts ", ...)):
    action = "tts_read_text"
    query = extracted_text
    → dispatch to TTSCog immediately

Phrases Matched: - "read this. text" → "read this text" (punctuation normalized) - "read this, text" → "read this text" - "read this: text" → normalized to "read this text" - "say this! text" → normalized - "tts text" → direct pass-through - "read that", "read the last message" → tts_read_last - "read brief", "listen to brief" → tts_read_brief

Why Pre-Classifier? - Eliminates 50-100ms of classifier latency for common voice use cases - Ensures "read this" feels instant (Discord UX) - Reduces API token spend (no brain call for trivial reads)

docs.title: File Structure
docs.text: File Purpose Lines modules/tts.py ElevenLabs API, voice rotation, MP3 generation ~210 cogs/tts_actions.py Discord action handlers (read text/last/URL/brief) ~220 bot.py (TTS intercept) Fast-path phrase recognition in on_message() ~70 data/tts/ Output directory for generated MP3 files — data/tts/voice_history.json Voice rotation state (persisted) — data/tts/latest_brief.md Morning brief text (used by tts_read_brief) —

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
Environment Variables: - ELEVENLABS_API_KEY — Required. Loaded from .env via _get_api_key()

Model Defaults: - DEFAULT_MODEL = "eleven_turbo_v2_5" (fast + cost-efficient) - Can override per call: text_to_speech(..., model="eleven_multilingual_v2")

Voice Settings: - stability=0.5 — Default (balanced) - similarity_boost=0.75 — Default (strong voice matching) - Customizable per call

Storage: - Voice history: data/tts/voice_history.json - MP3 output: data/tts/brief_<timestamp>.mp3 - Directory auto-created if missing

docs.title: Morning Brief
docs.text:
morning-brief calls morning_brief_audio() to render the daily briefing as audio. Results in scheduled podcast posted to Discord.

Flow: 1. Autonomy loop generates brief text → data/tts/latest_brief.md 2. morning_brief_audio(brief_text) converts to MP3 3. Brief is posted to Discord with audio attachment 4. Users can say "read brief" to replay

docs.title: Meeting Summaries
docs.text: TTS can render meeting summaries to audio for async listening. Not yet integrated into scheduled flows — see future-work for roadmap.

docs.title: Alert Sounds
docs.text:
speak_alert(text, channel) generates and posts audio alerts for: - High-priority candidate drops - Crash notifications - Important FORGE state changes

Example: 

await speak_alert("New candidate matched: Alice Chen", channel)

docs.title: Voice Gallery
docs.text:
ElevenLabs offers 30+ voices across accents (American, British, Indian, etc.) and genders. Full list fetched dynamically via get_available_voices().

Common Voices: - Adam (American male) - Alice (American female) - Luna (British female, distinctive) - Sophia (American female, warm) - Jackson (American male, deep) - Rachel (American female, professional)

Run /current-voice or check logs to see today's selected voice.

docs.title: Performance & Limits
docs.text: Metric Limit Notes Max text length ~5000 chars ElevenLabs API limit. TTS errors if exceeded. Output MP3 size < 10 MB Hard Discord file limit. Cog warns if exceeded. API rate ~100 req/min Based on subscription tier. No explicit rate limiting in code. Voice list cache Per request Fetched fresh from ElevenLabs every text_to_speech() call. Consider caching if high volume. File generation ~2-5s Depends on text length and network latency.

docs.title: Error Handling
docs.text: Error Handling Recovery Missing ELEVENLABS_API_KEY Raises ValueError Add API key to .env, restart bot ElevenLabs API returns non-200 Raises ValueError with HTTP status + error text Check API key, quota, network No voices available from API Raises ValueError Temporary API issue; retry MP3 > 10 MB User gets warning message Ask user to provide shorter text URL fetch timeout (15s) User gets "Couldn't fetch URL" URL may be slow or unresponsive Channel history empty (read_last) User gets "No previous message to read." Need at least one previous message

docs.title: Valorant & Gaming
docs.text:
4 min read · Last updated April 04, 2026 · 700 words

FORGE's Valorant API integration, esports data pipeline, and gaming activity tracking.

docs.title: Valorant API: Henrik's Unofficial API
docs.text: Real-time player stats, rank info, and match history via Henrik Dev API.

docs.title: Core Features
docs.text:
• Player Rank: competitive rank, RR (rating points), ELO, peak rank
  • Last Match: detailed stats (kills, deaths, assists, ACS, headshot %)
  • Match History: multi-match lookup with per-map breakdown
  • Account Info: player profile, region, agent list

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
from modules.valorant import get_rank, get_last_match, get_match_history

# Initialize with player defaults
init_valorant(api_key="...", default_name="Zack", default_tag="haha1")

# Get rank
rank_info = await get_rank()  # uses defaults
rank_info = await get_rank("Asuna", "main")  # specific player

# Match history (last 5 matches)
history = await get_match_history(count=5)

API: Async via requests in executor (non-blocking in Discord context).

docs.title: VLR.gg Esports Integration
docs.text: Professional Valorant esports data: player stats, team schedules, per-map breakdowns. Uses vlrdevapi (free, no auth).

docs.title: Data Sources
docs.text:
• VLR.gg: esports matches, results, player averages
  • PrizePicks API: unauthenticated current projection lines
  • Auto-Grade: match player picks against actual VLR results

docs.title: Functions
docs.text: Function Purpose search_player(name) Find player by IGN, returns player_id + country get_player_profile(player_id) Name, team, country, peak rating get_player_map_kills(player_id, map_name) Per-map kill count (last N matches) get_player_averages(player_id) K/D/A, ACS, kills per round (cached 30min) search_matches(date_range) Upcoming/completed matches get_pp_lines() Current PrizePicks Valorant prop lines

docs.title: Caching
docs.text:
• Player ID: in-memory cache (frequent lookups)
  • Averages: 30min TTL (avoid re-hitting VLR)
  • Lines: fresh on each call (PrizePicks updates frequently)

docs.title: Valorant Lineups Database
docs.text: Lineup videos/GIFs for agents on specific maps and sites.

docs.title: Agent Coverage
docs.text: 26 agents with full ability mappings: - Sentinels: Cypher, Killjoy, Chamber - Duelists: Jett, Reyna, Phoenix, Yoru, Neon - Initiators: Sova, Breach, Skye, Fade, Gekko, Kay/O - Controllers: Viper, Omen, Brimstone, Astra, Clove, Iso, Deadlock

docs.title: Storage
docs.text: SQLite forge.db table: valorant_lineups - Agent, map, site (A/B/mid) - Ability name - Video URL (YouTube/Invidious) - Notes, saved date

docs.title: Endpoints
docs.text:
await search_lineups("sova", "haven", "a site", "recon bolt")
→ returns [{"url": "...", "title": "...", "description": "..."}]

await save_lineup(url, agent, map_name, site, ability, notes="")
await get_saved_lineups(agent, map_name)
await delete_lineup(lineup_id)

docs.title: Val Swarm: 11-Agent Predictor
docs.text: Tiered analysis for PrizePicks Valorant props. Runs on VPS only (FORGE_INSTANCE == "vps").

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
Tier 1 (8 agents, pure math, $0): - Form, Map, Matchup, Role, FirstBlood, Variance, Rounds, OpponentStrength

Tier 2 (2 agents, Haiku, ~$0.01): - EdgeFinder, DevilsAdvocate

Tier 3 (1 agent, Haiku, ~$0.01): - Synthesizer (leader, synthesizes all signals)

docs.title: Cost & Data
docs.text:
• Total: ~$0.02-0.03 per full analysis
  • Data: $0 (VLR + PrizePicks free APIs)
  • Output: per-player verdict (over/under recommendation + confidence)

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
from core.val_swarm import run_swarm_analysis

# Analyze all current lines
results = await run_swarm_analysis()

# Specific players
results = await run_swarm_analysis(players=["starxo", "Asuna"])

docs.title: Game Session Tracker
docs.text: Monitors Discord presence for gaming activity (Valorant, etc.). Logs to SQLite with weekly summaries.

docs.title: Tracking
docs.text:
• Requires: presences + members privileged intents
  • Trigger: presence update → detect game start/stop
  • Storage: game_sessions table
  • game_name, started_at, ended_at, duration_minutes

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
await on_presence_update(before_member, after_member)
→ auto-detects Valorant starts/stops

await get_session_summary(days=7)
→ weekly gaming summary
